article_title: Engineered E. Coli Converts Plastic Bottle Waste into Parkinson's Drug in Bioengineering First

content_preview.title: Engineered E. Coli Converts Plastic Bottle Waste into Parkinson's Drug in Bioengineering First
content_preview.summary: University of Edinburgh researchers engineered bacteria to transform PET plastic into levodopa, a frontline Parkinson's medication, achieving 84 percent conversion from industrial waste in a proof-of-concept published in Nature Sustainability.
content_preview.body_excerpt:
## Overview

Researchers at the University of Edinburgh have engineered *Escherichia coli* bacteria to convert polyethylene terephthalate (PET) plastic waste into levodopa (L-DOPA), the primary medication used to treat Parkinson's disease. The work, [published in *Nature Sustainability*](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41893-026-01785-z) on March 16, represents what the team describes as the first use of engineering biology to transform plastic waste into a therapeutic compound for neurological...

editor_notes.content_quality: Outstanding synthetic biology article with detailed metabolic pathway description and honest assessment of scalability challenges.
editor_notes.source_verification: Edinburgh confirms Wallace, C-Loop £14M, Nature Sustainability March 16, UKRI/EPSRC/IBioIC funding, Wallace quote verbatim. Interesting Engineering confirms 50M tonnes PET, Deane quote. Specific metrics (84% conversion, 5.0 g/L, 100+ doses) attributed to Edinburgh/Nature paper. Phys.org archived but confirms two-strain approach and Wallace scalability quote.
editor_notes.factual_accuracy: Core claims verified across multiple sources. Technical details about metabolic pathway species are consistent with synthetic biology conventions.
editor_notes.tone_assessment: Neutral with appropriate scientific rigor. Good balance between breakthrough framing and honest limitations.
editor_notes.originality: No prior coverage of plastic-to-pharmaceutical conversion in the archive.
editor_notes.overall_assessment: Excellent synthetic biology article with strong sourcing. Approved.
